slides.title: Overview
slides.text: The legendary Boathouse restaurant needed a new site, but the requirements were challenging. Staff needed an easy way to add new promotions, update the event calendar, and update the menu. In addition, with no parking at the restaurant, a custom transit and parking map was needed.

slides.title: Previous Site
slides.text: The previous website did not reflect the vibrancy of the brand, nor the high activity level with live music and other events. There was no event calendar – only a few promotion slides. Parking information was inadequate, with no map or transit info. All updates required a developer, causing delays.

slides.title: Wireframes & Schema
slides.text: After meeting with stakeholders and reviewing requirements, I created some wireframes to test different layout ideas. We validated these with the client before showing them high-fidelity mock-ups. These frames also helped us define the schema for the Storyblok CMS — the blocks of content that would be editable.

slides.title: Branding Challenges
slides.text: The Boathouse branding could best be described as ‘busy’! I tested all the colour combinations for contrast (both mathematical and perceived) to select the best for different UI elements such as buttons, titles and links. I created a palette of different tints and shades while boosting vibrance. To improve legibility, I selected DM Sans for body copy, and kept brand fonts only for selected titles.

slides.title: Custom Map
slides.text: The client wanted a stripped-down map showing only essential info for transit and parking. I exported OpenStreetMap data, converted it to SVG, then painstakingly removed unnecessary layers, fixed and added labels, recoloured elements to match the brand, added custom icons and marked free and paid parking lots. I added a detailed legend with recommended parking spots and walking times to the restaurant.

slides.title: Menu Complexity
slides.text: I wanted to keep the look and feel of the printed menu, but make it easier to read and navigate on the web, including mobile devices. DM Sans has a larger x-height than the printed menu font, making it more legible, especially at small sizes. I had to reorganize and standardize parts of the menu so they would work with the CMS. For mobile, the categories scroll horizontally, following the convention of apps such as Skip the Dishes.

slides.title: Event Calendar
slides.text: Events needed a compact version for the calendar on the home page, and a detailed view on the Events page. Events could be recurring, ticketed or cancelled. The calendar also needed to show food and drink deals, special events and closures. It was a challenge to keep the calendar from becoming cluttered. I used icons and colour for different categories and was careful to ensure the small text was reasonably legible.

slides.title: Overview
slides.text: RouteThis hired us to help design and develop “Certify,” a mobile app that ISP field technicians use to optimize WiFi equipment placement. Techs needed a standardized tool; the business needed a guided, on-site, data-capturing workflow. I was the sole product designer over a 12-month period, taking a complex tool from concept to near-launch.

slides.title: Challenges
slides.text: Designing for technicians in the field meant the app had to be very user-friendly and efficient, as they often work in high-pressure situations. The product owner had sketched out a rough workflow, but the concept was untested: an app that maps your steps while measuring WiFi signal as you walk from room to room. While the devs worked on a proof-of-concept, I iterated on the PM’s wireframes in Miro, asking questions and doing more research, before moving on to high-fidelity mockups in Figma.

slides.title: Steps
slides.text: The first version had these main steps: 1. Technician sign in 2. Select new or existing installation 3. Location details 4. Place router 5. Place mesh pods on each floor as needed based on signal strength 6. Take photos of router and pods 7. Test speed at router 8. Walk to each room and test speed 9. Certify (customer receives report)

slides.title: Prototypes
slides.text: The app went through many changes as we tested each step. For example, we added the ability to edit pod locations, and changed “Speed Test” to “Wireless Performance.” Here are a few examples of the prototypes I made in Figma to demonstrate how interactions and flows should work.

slides.title: WiFi Mapping
slides.text: The original version generated a signal heat map as the user walked, but this was unnecessary and added clutter. I replaced the green blobs with dashed lines. Then, to increase efficiency, we had the user select the room type *while* performance was being measured instead of after. In the second version, I used icons instead of labels to indicate the room type.

slides.title: Apartment Units
slides.text: The biggest design challenge came in phase two of the project. The app relied on map data and the device compass to orient and locate the user inside the building. But what if the installation was in an apartment that didn’t match the building footprint? I designed four versions of this feature and tested prototypes with people unfamiliar with the project. They were confused and unable to select the correct shape and orientation in early designs due to inadequate visual information. The second prototype here makes it easier to position the unit within the building.

slides.title: Localization
slides.text: Preparing the app for international markets presented new challenges. It had to work in 13 languages or dialects, support configurable terminology and abbreviations, and allow variables in the middle of strings. I worked with translators and developers to ensure consistency, tested constantly on iOS and Android, and adjusted the design as needed to accommodate longer strings required by most European languages. Finnish and Greek were especially tricky!

slides.title: Administration & Results
slides.text:
The system required an admin interface to set up ISPs as tenants, and to analyze data from technicians to track quality of installations over time. I designed and contributed code to the initial version of this UI.

Certify remains a core product for RouteThis, credited by clients with providing an “efficient installation experience” for their techs, allowing better customer service and reducing service calls.

name: Festival Marketing Sites
alt: screenshots of music festival web sites

slides.title: Overview
slides.text: Farm League Brewing runs two music festivals in Ontario each year: Fest2Fall in Cambridge and Meadows in Fergus. Festivals need a solid online presence, with easy navigation, helpful content, and effective SEO to guide people to the right details. Strata worked with Farm League to build sites that reflect the scale of their events while improving discoverability and site management.

slides.title: Challenges
slides.text: When Farm League launched their festivals in 2023, their designer used Wix to create single-page websites. The Fest2Fall site had the issues such as: 1. Large chunks of text were in images and thus inaccessible to screen-readers and search engine crawlers. 2. Decorative elements on the sides blocked the main content area at medium window sizes. The page was optimized for phones and for wide screens, but nothing in between. 3. More pages were needed for artist bios and the festival schedule. Separate pages would also improve search rankings for specific topics.

slides.title: Redesigning Fest2Fall
slides.text: One page became six. I used motifs from the existing design throughout the site: the wavy line for separators and coloured bubbles for call-to-action buttons in the middle of the home page. To address text in images, I used web versions of the two main fonts where feasible. Some text was kept as images, but split into smaller SVG’s with the appropriate <title> text added for SEO and accessibility. This also allowed discrete links to be added – to the sponsors from the text chunks, and to the Artists page from the separate images.
slides.linkText: See my blog post for more details

slides.title: Annual Redesigns
slides.text: Fest2Fall 2024 was a great success, and soon it was time to plan for Meadows 2025. We used Fest2Fall as a template and made small improvements to both sites along with each year’s redesign. In the first year, Farm League asked us to delete all the content when the festival was over. I proposed that we keep most of the content up throughout the year, resulting in much better search results. I also adding structured event data (schema.org). These efforts helped to drive a 39% increase in page views, 20% more ticket sales, and 85K active users on Meadows in 2025.

slides.title: Freshly Baked Cookies
slides.text: As Fest2Fall 2025 approached, Farm League requested that we add Meta tracking to further their marketing efforts. Of course, this requires the user’s consent. Nobody likes these banners, so after some research and brainstorming, I proposed a different approach: 1. Don't show the banner until the user scrolls part way down the screen. 2. Frame the use of cookies in a way that is more likely to be accepted – promoting Canadian artists. 3. Clearly explain cookies on the Privacy Policy and allow the visitor to change their consent.

slides.title: Overview
slides.text: The Strata team embarked on an internal project to speed up UI development. We experimented with a standardized way of creating themeable components in Figma and automatically generating code using Figma’s API. (This was before AI tools had developed sufficiently for this type of thing.)

slides.title: Palette Generator
slides.text: My first contribution was to research the art and science of colour palette creation. I wanted our components to be themeable in a way that maintained consistent contrast (change colours with no change in contrast). Traditional colour models (RGB, HSL) are useless for this, so I experimented with formulae to calculate *perceived* lightness. I built a palette generator that takes a key colour in HSL and generates a shade or tint as close as possible to a target preceived lightness (95% and 25% in the examples shown).
slides.linkText: See my blog post on colour

slides.title: Standardizing Components
slides.text: The next stage was to create the components in Figma in a consistent, scalable way. This would make code generation easier while allowing designers to build and customize. I researched design tokens and naming conventions and the team agreed on a system. I created the components and documentation while a developer worked on a way to generate code that minimized CSS conflicts and duplication across variants.

slides.title: Accessible & Optimized Code
slides.text: As an expert on semantic and accessible HTML, I provided code samples of each component for the developers to shoot for. Later, I worked directly on the TypeScript code to improve the output.

name: AI-Powered Tenure App
role: UX Design, Front-End Dev, Prompt Engineering

slides.title: Overview
slides.text: McMaster’s Department of Medicine had a laborious process for faculty who wanted to apply for promotion or tenure. The process took over 30 hours, plus effort on the part of the review board and others. It was mainly a lot of summarizing of previously written documentation. The goal of this project was to streamline the process and reduce that time from 30 to less than 2 hours, and leverage LLMs to do it.

slides.title: Web App
slides.text:
We built a web app that takes candidates through 8 steps to gather the information needed to generate the required documents. The information requested depends on the faculty type. Progress is saved automatically so that users can leave and return to the process as needed.

I worked on a small team with a product manager and one other developer. We chose the Shadcn library as a basis for the UI to avoid inventing a design system from scratch. I customized it in Figma based on McMaster’s branding.

slides.title: Steps
slides.text: I haven’t included every step of the application, just some of the inital mock-ups. I enjoy creating clean and effective tables and forms — bad form UX is a pet peeve!

slides.title: Career Highlights
slides.text: It was essential for the applicant to specify highlights of their career so that the AI would know what to emphasize in the generated Personal Statement. I made each section collapsible to avoid visual clutter

slides.title: Error Correction
slides.text: The first version required users to complete each step before moving on. Later I realized it would be better to allow users to move back and forth on steps 1–5 until the information is actually needed — step 6 where the first document is generated. If the user hasn’t completed one or more previous steps, the error message provides details and takes them directly to the first step that has missing information.

slides.title: Generative AI Challenges
slides.text: The team was new to using AI, and there was much trial and error to get consistent results. For example, the sample documents we asked the AI to use as templates used incorrect markdown syntax at first. The documents were generated in markdown, then converted to HTML for editing by the user, and finally to Microsoft Word format for downloading. I helped to improve the AI prompts and the conversion processes – including recreating the required McMaster letterhead in HTML in a way that the Word converter could use.

slides.title: Administration
slides.text: The app required an admin interface so that authorized members of the review board could add candidates and review the submitted documents. The system kept track of the status of each application as it moved through multiple review steps.

slides.title: Candidate Profile
slides.text: In addition to generating three documents, the system allowed the review board to compile other needed documents, store them in one place, and download the final package.
